FastAPI is a modern Python API framework built around Python type hints. It supports automatic validation, OpenAPI docs, dependency injection, async endpoints, and high-performance ASGI servers.

Response serialization

FastAPI middleware
 ↓
Dependency resolution
 ↓
Validation via Pydantic
 ↓
Path operation function

class UserCreate(BaseModel):
 name: str

def get_user(

):

def create_user(payload: UserCreate):

 return {"id": 1}

@app.get("/me")
def me(user = Depends(get_current_user)):
 return user

## 34. PostgreSQL Basics

PostgreSQL is relational. Use it when data has clear relationships, strong consistency needs, transactions, joins, reporting, and constraints.

### Core concepts

- Table

- Row

- Column

- Primary key

- Foreign key

- Index

- Constraint

- Transaction

- View

- Join

### Example schema

```
CREATE TABLE users (
 id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
 email TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
 name TEXT NOT NULL,
 created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE orders (
 id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
 user_id BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
 total_amount NUMERIC(10, 2) NOT NULL,
 status TEXT NOT NULL,
 created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

```

 id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,

 name TEXT NOT NULL,

);

 id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
 user_id BIGINT NOT NULL REFERENCES users(id),
 total_amount NUMERIC(10, 2) NOT NULL,

);

### Joins

```
SELECT u.email, o.total_amount
FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE o.status = 'PAID';

```

FROM users u
JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id
WHERE o.status = 'PAID';

### Transactions

```
BEGIN;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2;
COMMIT;

```

UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1;
UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 100 WHERE id = 2;

ROLLBACK;

## 35. PostgreSQL Indexing

Indexes speed up reads but slow down writes and consume storage.

```
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_id ON orders(user_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_created_at ON orders(status, created_at DESC);

```

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_user_id ON orders(user_id);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_created_at ON orders(status, created_at DESC);

### When to add index

- Columns used in WHERE

WHERE

- Foreign keys

- Join columns

- Sorting columns

- High-cardinality columns

### When index may not help

- Very small table

- Low-cardinality boolean column

- Query returns large part of table

- Function on indexed column without functional index

Bad:

```
WHERE LOWER(email) = 'a@example.com'

```

WHERE LOWER(email) = 'a@example.com'

Better:

```
CREATE INDEX idx_users_lower_email ON users(LOWER(email));

```

CREATE INDEX idx_users_lower_email ON users(LOWER(email));

### EXPLAIN

```
EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 10;

```

EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 10;

Use it to see whether indexes are used and where time is spent.

## 36. MongoDB Basics

MongoDB stores documents in collections.

Example document:

```
{
 "_id": "...",

 "email": "amit@example.com",
 "addresses": [
 { "city": "Delhi", "type": "home" }
 ]
}

{
 "_id": "...",
 "name": "Amit",
 "email": "amit@example.com",
 "addresses": [
 { "city": "Delhi", "type": "home" }
 ]
}

Use MongoDB when:

- Data is document-shaped.

- Flexible schema is helpful.

- You often load whole aggregate documents.

- Rapid product iteration matters.

Use PostgreSQL when:

- Strong relationships matter.

- Complex joins/reporting matter.

- Strict constraints and transactions are important.

```
db.orders.aggregate([

])

$match

$project

$group

- $sort : sort

$sort

$lookup

- $unwind : flatten arrays

Performance tips:

- Put $match early.

## 38. SQL vs NoSQL Interview Answer

> I use SQL when relationships, constraints, joins, and transactional consistency are important. I use MongoDB when data is naturally document-oriented, schema changes frequently, and most reads/writes happen around a complete aggregate. I would not choose MongoDB just because it is flexible; I would choose it if the access pattern fits document storage.

I use SQL when relationships, constraints, joins, and transactional consistency are important. I use MongoDB when data is naturally document-oriented, schema changes frequently, and most reads/writes happen around a complete aggregate. I would not choose MongoDB just because it is flexible; I would choose it if the access pattern fits document storage.

# PART F — Event-Driven Systems, Webhooks, Queues

## 39. Why Event-Driven Architecture

Synchronous flow:

```
API receives request -> does all work immediately -> returns response

```

API receives request -> does all work immediately -> returns response

Problem:

- Slow response

- external API failure affects user request

- hard to retry

- tightly coupled

Event-driven flow:

```
API receives request -> saves DB record -> publishes event/job -> returns response
Worker processes event -> updates DB -> sends notification

```

API receives request -> saves DB record -> publishes event/job -> returns response
Worker processes event -> updates DB -> sends notification

Benefits:

- Decoupling

- Better scalability

- Retry handling

- Background processing

- Better user experience

Tradeoffs:

- Eventual consistency

- Duplicate events

- Ordering issues

- More infra and debugging complexity

## 40. Webhooks

A webhook is an HTTP callback from one system to another.

Example: Payment provider sends:

```
POST /webhooks/payment

```

POST /webhooks/payment

Payload:

```
{
 "event_id": "evt_123",
 "type": "payment.success",
 "order_id": "ord_1",
 "amount": 1000
}

```

{
 "event_id": "evt_123",
 "type": "payment.success",
 "order_id": "ord_1",
 "amount": 1000
}

### Webhook handling checklist

- Verify signature.

- Parse event.

- Check if event already processed.

- Store raw event.

- Process idempotently.

- Return 2xx quickly.

- Push heavy work to queue.

### Idempotency table

```
CREATE TABLE processed_webhooks (
 event_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
 processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

```

CREATE TABLE processed_webhooks (
 event_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
 processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

Pseudo-code:

```
def handle_webhook(event):
 if already_processed(event.id):
 return "ok"

 save_event(event)
 enqueue_job(event)
 mark_processed(event.id)

```

def handle_webhook(event):
 if already_processed(event.id):
 return "ok"

 save_event(event)
 enqueue_job(event)
 mark_processed(event.id)

Important:

Webhook providers retry. Your handler must survive duplicate delivery.

## 41. Queue Concepts

A queue stores work to be processed later.

```
Producer -> Queue -> Consumer/Worker

```

Producer -> Queue -> Consumer/Worker

Use cases:

- email sending

- invoice generation

- webhook processing

- image/video processing

- notification delivery

- report generation

- retrying failed external API calls

Important concepts:

- Job

- Producer

- Worker/consumer

- Retry

- Dead-letter queue

- Delay

- Backoff

- Visibility timeout

- Idempotency

### At-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once

Semantics

Meaning

Risk

at-most-once

delivered zero or one time

message may be lost

at-least-once

delivered one or more times

duplicates possible

exactly-once

effectively once

hard and system-specific

In practical systems, assume at-least-once and make consumers idempotent.

## 42. Redis

Redis is often used for:

- Cache

- Session store

- Rate limiting

- Pub/Sub

- Streams

- Distributed locks

- Job queues backing store

### Cache example

```
def get_user(user_id):
 key = f"user:{user_id}"
 cached = redis.get(key)
 if cached:
 return json.loads(cached)

 user = db.fetch_user(user_id)
 redis.setex(key, 300, json.dumps(user))
 return user

```

def get_user(user_id):
 key = f"user:{user_id}"
 cached = redis.get(key)
 if cached:
 return json.loads(cached)

 user = db.fetch_user(user_id)
 redis.setex(key, 300, json.dumps(user))
 return user

### Cache invalidation

Hardest part of caching.

Strategies:

- TTL-based expiry

- delete/update cache on write

- write-through cache

- cache-aside pattern

### Rate limiting idea

```
key = rate:user:{user_id}:minute:{timestamp_minute}
increment key
expire key after 60 seconds
if count > limit: reject

```

key = rate:user:{user_id}:minute:{timestamp_minute}
increment key
expire key after 60 seconds
if count > limit: reject

## 43. Kafka

Kafka is an event streaming platform.

Core terms:

- Producer: writes events

- Consumer: reads events

- Topic: named event stream

- Partition: split of topic for parallelism

- Offset: consumer position

- Consumer group: consumers sharing work

- Broker: Kafka server

Use Kafka when:

- High-throughput event streams

- Multiple consumers need same events

- Durable event log

- Replay capability

- Analytics/event pipelines

Do not use Kafka for simple one-off background jobs if Redis/BullMQ/Celery is enough.

## 44. BullMQ/Celery-style Job Queues

BullMQ is common in Node.js, backed by Redis. In Python, Celery/RQ/Arq are common patterns.

Job queue features:

- delayed jobs

- retries

- repeatable jobs

- concurrency

- job progress

- failed jobs

Example conceptual flow:

```
POST /orders
 -> save order PENDING
 -> add process_payment job
 -> return order id

worker process_payment
 -> call payment API
 -> update order PAID/FAILED
 -> enqueue send_receipt job

```

## 51. React Practical Patterns

### Debounce search

```
function useDebouncedValue<T>(value: T, delay: number) {
 const [debounced, setDebounced] = useState(value);

 useEffect(() => {
 const id = setTimeout(() => setDebounced(value), delay);
 return () => clearTimeout(id);
 }, [value, delay]);

 return debounced;
}

```

function useDebouncedValue<T>(value: T, delay: number) {
 const [debounced, setDebounced] = useState(value);

 useEffect(() => {
 const id = setTimeout(() => setDebounced(value), delay);
 return () => clearTimeout(id);
 }, [value, delay]);

 return debounced;
}

Usage:

```
const debouncedSearch = useDebouncedValue(search, 400);

useEffect(() => {
 fetch(`/api/users?search=${debouncedSearch}`);
}, [debouncedSearch]);

```

 fetch(`/api/users?search=${debouncedSearch}`);
}, [debouncedSearch]);

}

# PART I — Redux and Redux Toolkit

## 57. Why Redux

Redux is for predictable global state management.

Use Redux for:

- Auth user state

- app-wide preferences

- complex shared state

- data used by many unrelated components

- state transitions that benefit from logs/devtools

Do not use Redux for:

- every input field

- purely local modal open/close state

- state used by one component only

## 58. Redux Core Concepts

```
UI dispatches action -> reducer updates store -> UI re-renders from selected state

```

UI dispatches action -> reducer updates store -> UI re-renders from selected state

Terms:

- Store: global state container

- Action: event object

- Reducer: pure function that updates state

- Dispatch: send action

- Selector: read state

## 59. Redux Toolkit Slice

```
import { createSlice, PayloadAction } from "@reduxjs/toolkit";

type AuthState = {
 token: string | null;
 user: { id: number; name: string } | null;
};

const initialState: AuthState = {
 token: null,
 user: null,
};

const authSlice = createSlice({
 name: "auth",
 initialState,
 reducers: {
 setCredentials(state, action: PayloadAction<AuthState>) {
 state.token = action.payload.token;
 state.user = action.payload.user;
 },
 logout(state) {
 state.token = null;
 state.user = null;
 },
 },
});

export const { setCredentials, logout } = authSlice.actions;
export default authSlice.reducer;

```

 state.user = action.payload.user;
 },
 logout(state) {
 state.token = null;
 state.user = null;
 },
 },
});

export const { setCredentials, logout } = authSlice.actions;
export default authSlice.reducer;

Redux Toolkit uses Immer internally, so direct-looking mutation is converted into immutable updates.

## 62. RTK Query

RTK Query is useful for server-state management: fetching, caching, refetching, invalidation.

```
import { createApi, fetchBaseQuery } from "@reduxjs/toolkit/query/react";

export const api = createApi({
 reducerPath: "api",
 baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({ baseUrl: "/api" }),
 tagTypes: ["User"],
 endpoints: builder => ({
 getUsers: builder.query<User[], void>({
 query: () => "/users",
 providesTags: ["User"],
 }),
 createUser: builder.mutation<User, Partial<User>>({
 query: body => ({
 url: "/users",
 method: "POST",
 body,
 }),
 invalidatesTags: ["User"],
 }),
 }),
});

export const { useGetUsersQuery, useCreateUserMutation } = api;

```

# PART J — Full-Stack Feature Architecture

## 63. Example Feature: Order Creation

### Requirement

User creates an order. Payment is processed asynchronously. User sees live status.

### Flow

```
React form
 ↓ POST /orders
FastAPI validates request
 ↓
Auth dependency checks user
 ↓
PostgreSQL transaction creates order PENDING
 ↓
Queue job: process_payment
 ↓
API returns order_id
 ↓
Worker calls payment provider
 ↓
Webhook confirms payment
 ↓
DB updates order PAID
 ↓
Frontend polls or receives WebSocket update

```

React form
 ↓ POST /orders
FastAPI validates request
 ↓
Auth dependency checks user
 ↓
PostgreSQL transaction creates order PENDING
 ↓
Queue job: process_payment
 ↓
API returns order_id
 ↓
Worker calls payment provider
 ↓
Webhook confirms payment
 ↓
DB updates order PAID
 ↓
Frontend polls or receives WebSocket update

### Why not process payment inside request?

Because payment API may be slow/unreliable. The user request should not block for long. Async processing enables retries and better failure handling.

### Failure cases

Failure

Handling

API validation fails

return 422/400

user unauthorized

401/403

DB insert fails

rollback

queue publish fails

transaction/outbox pattern

payment API timeout

retry with backoff

duplicate webhook

idempotency key/event table

frontend refresh

fetch order status by ID

## 64. Outbox Pattern

Problem:

```
Save DB record succeeds, queue publish fails.

```

Save DB record succeeds, queue publish fails.

Then system state becomes inconsistent.

Outbox solution:

- In same DB transaction, save business record and outbox event.

- Separate worker reads unsent outbox events.

- Publishes to queue/broker.

- Marks outbox event sent.

Schema:

```
CREATE TABLE outbox_events (
 id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
 event_type TEXT NOT NULL,
 payload JSONB NOT NULL,
 status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'PENDING',
 created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
 sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);

```

CREATE TABLE outbox_events (
 id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
 event_type TEXT NOT NULL,
 payload JSONB NOT NULL,
 status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'PENDING',
 created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
 sent_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);

## 65. API Design Checklist

For every API endpoint, ask:

- What resource does it represent?

- What method is correct?

- What status code should be returned?

- What validation is needed?

- Who is authorized?

- Is it idempotent?

- How is pagination handled?

- What errors can happen?

- What logs/metrics are needed?

- What DB indexes support it?

## 66. Production Debugging Method

When debugging production issues, do not randomly change code.

Use this flow:

- Reproduce or understand exact symptoms.

- Identify scope: one user, many users, one endpoint, all endpoints.

- Check recent deployments/config changes.

- Trace request ID across logs.

- Check API response codes and latency.

- Check DB slow queries/errors.

- Check queue backlog/retries/dead letters.

- Check external API status/rate limits.

- Form hypothesis.

- Test hypothesis.

- Fix smallest safe part.

- Add regression test/monitoring.

Interview phrase:

> I first narrow down whether it is frontend, API, database, queue, or third-party failure. Then I follow a request ID/data ID through logs and state changes instead of guessing.

I first narrow down whether it is frontend, API, database, queue, or third-party failure. Then I follow a request ID/data ID through logs and state changes instead of guessing.

# PART L — Interview Practical Questions

## 69. Python Questions

### Q1. Difference between list and tuple?

List is mutable; tuple is immutable. Use list for changing collections and tuple for fixed records or hashable keys.

### Q2. What is GIL?

The Global Interpreter Lock allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time in standard CPython. It affects CPU-bound threaded Python code. I/O-bound threading can still help because threads release the GIL while waiting on I/O.

### Q3. What are decorators?

Decorators wrap functions/classes to add reusable behavior like auth, logging, timing, caching.

### Q4. What is a generator?

A generator yields values lazily, useful for large data and streaming.

### Q5. What is asyncio ?

asyncio

A library/event-loop model for concurrent I/O using async/await. It is useful when many tasks spend time waiting on I/O.

## 70. Flask/FastAPI Questions

### Q1. Flask vs FastAPI?

Flask is minimal and flexible WSGI framework. FastAPI is ASGI-first, type-hint based, with automatic validation/docs and strong async support.

### Q2. What is middleware?

Code that runs before/after request handling. Used for logging, auth, CORS, timing, request IDs, security headers.

### Q3. What is dependency injection in FastAPI?

A way to define reusable dependency functions/classes that FastAPI resolves for endpoints, commonly used for auth, DB sessions, and shared validation.

### Q4. Why response models?

They ensure consistent response shape and prevent leaking internal fields.

### Q5. What happens if you use blocking calls inside async route?

It can block the event loop and reduce concurrency. Use async libraries or offload blocking work.

## 71. React Questions

### Q1. What triggers re-render?

State change, parent re-render, context change, selected external store state change.

### Q2. What is useEffect?

A hook for synchronizing a component with external systems such as API calls, subscriptions, timers, DOM APIs.

### Q3. Why cleanup in useEffect?

To avoid memory leaks, duplicate subscriptions, stale timers, and state updates after unmount.

### Q4. Controlled vs uncontrolled components?

Controlled inputs store value in React state. Uncontrolled inputs store value in DOM and are accessed through refs.

### Q5. What is key in list rendering?

A stable identity for React to correctly reconcile list items. Avoid array index as key when items can reorder/delete.

## 72. TypeScript Questions

### Q1. Why TypeScript?

It catches many errors before runtime, improves refactoring, editor support, and API contracts.

### Q2. Type vs interface?

Both define object shapes. Interface is extendable and suited for object contracts. Type supports unions, primitives, mapped/conditional types.

### Q3. What is generics?

A way to write reusable type-safe functions/components/classes.

### Q4. What is discriminated union?

A union type with a common field that allows safe narrowing, useful for loading/success/error states.

## 73. Redux Questions

### Q1. When should you use Redux?

When state is shared across many unrelated components, has complex transitions, or benefits from predictable updates/devtools.

### Q2. What is reducer?

A pure function that computes next state from previous state and action.

### Q3. Why Redux Toolkit?

It reduces boilerplate, gives good defaults, uses Immer, and includes tools for async and API state management.

### Q4. Redux vs Context?

Context passes values through tree and triggers consumers on change. Redux gives centralized state, predictable updates, middleware, devtools, and better patterns for complex state.

## 74. Database Questions

### Q1. SQL vs NoSQL?

SQL is best for relational data, constraints, joins, and transactions. NoSQL/document DB is best for flexible document-shaped data and aggregate-oriented access patterns.

### Q2. What is index?

A data structure that speeds up lookups but adds write/storage overhead.

### Q3. How do you optimize slow SQL query?

Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE, check indexes, reduce returned rows, avoid unnecessary joins, optimize filters, update statistics, and review schema/access patterns.

### Q4. MongoDB aggregation pipeline?

A sequence of stages where each stage transforms documents. Used for filtering, grouping, joining, sorting, projections, analytics.

## 75. Event-Driven Questions

### Q1. Why use queues?

To decouple slow/retryable work from user-facing requests and process tasks reliably in background.

### Q2. What is idempotency?

Repeating the same operation produces the same final result. Critical for retries and webhooks.

### Q3. How do you handle duplicate webhook events?

Verify signature, store event ID, check if already processed, process idempotently, return success for duplicates.

### Q4. Kafka vs Redis queue?

Kafka is durable event streaming with replay and consumer groups. Redis queues are simpler for background jobs. Choose based on scale, replay needs, and architecture complexity.

# PART P — Must-Know One-Liners

- REST is about resources, not actions.

- 401 means not authenticated; 403 means authenticated but not allowed.

- JWT is signed, not encrypted by default.

- Cookies need HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite for safer browser auth.

- Async helps with I/O-bound concurrency, not CPU-heavy parallelism.

- Indexes speed reads but slow writes and take storage.

- Webhooks must be idempotent because providers retry.

- Queues decouple slow/retryable work from request-response flow.

- React re-renders when state/props/context/external selected store changes.

- useEffect is for external synchronization, not general calculation.

useEffect

- Redux is for shared complex state, not every piece of local UI state.

- TypeScript helps make API contracts and UI props safer.

- Cache invalidation is a design problem, not just a Redis command.

- Production debugging is tracing flow, not guessing.

@app.route('/login', methods=['GET', 'POST'])

 if not password:

 return redirect(url_for('dashboard'))

## PRG pattern: Post → Redirect → Get

Problem: if a user refreshes after POST, browser may resubmit the form.

Solution: after successful POST, redirect to a GET page.

```
@app.post('/tasks')
def create_task():
 title = request.form['title']
 # save task
 flash('Task created', 'success')
 return redirect(url_for('list_tasks')) # PRG

```

@app.post('/tasks')
def create_task():
 title = request.form['title']
 # save task
 flash('Task created', 'success')
 return redirect(url_for('list_tasks')) # PRG

Interview line:

> I use PRG after successful form submissions so refresh/back does not repeat writes.

I use PRG after successful form submissions so refresh/back does not repeat writes.

## Sessions and cookies

Flask’s default session stores signed session data in the browser cookie. The user can see the data, but cannot modify it without invalidating the signature.

```
app.secret_key = 'use-env-secret-in-real-app'

@app.post('/login')
def login():
 session['user_id'] = user.id
 session['role'] = user.role
 return redirect(url_for('dashboard'))

@app.post('/logout')
def logout():
 session.clear()
 return redirect(url_for('login'))

```

app.secret_key = 'use-env-secret-in-real-app'

@app.post('/login')
def login():
 session['user_id'] = user.id
 session['role'] = user.role
 return redirect(url_for('dashboard'))

@app.post('/logout')
def logout():
 session.clear()
 return redirect(url_for('login'))

```
app.config.update(
 SESSION_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=True,
 SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True, # True in HTTPS production
 SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax',
)

```

app.config.update(

 SESSION_COOKIE_SECURE=True, # True in HTTPS production
 SESSION_COOKIE_SAMESITE='Lax',

Flag

Why it matters

HttpOnly

JS cannot read the cookie, reducing token theft through XSS

Secure

Browser sends cookie only over HTTPS

SameSite=Lax/Strict

Helps reduce CSRF risk

## Flask-Login frontend pattern

Flask-Login gives you current_user in views and templates.

current_user

```
from flask_login import LoginManager, UserMixin, login_user, logout_user, login_required, current_user

login_manager = LoginManager(app)
login_manager.login_view = 'login'

class User(UserMixin):
 def __init__(self, id, email):
 self.id = str(id)
 self.email = email

@login_manager.user_loader
def load_user(user_id):
 return get_user_by_id(user_id)

@app.post('/login')
def login():
 user = authenticate(request.form['email'], request.form['password'])
 if not user:
 flash('Invalid credentials', 'error')
 return redirect(url_for('login'))
 login_user(user)
 return redirect(url_for('dashboard'))

@app.get('/dashboard')
@login_required
def dashboard():
 return render_template('dashboard.html')

## Interview Q&A

Q: Why redirect after POST? A: To prevent duplicate submissions on refresh and to give the user a clean GET URL after mutation.

Q: Where should auth state live in Flask templates? A: In server-side user/session context such as current_user or session , not in hidden inputs or localStorage.

current_user

session

Q: Session vs JWT in Flask frontend? A: For server-rendered Flask, secure cookies/sessions are natural. JWT is more common for separate frontend/mobile/API clients.

HTML form must use enctype="multipart/form-data" .

```

</form>

```
from pathlib import Path
from werkzeug.utils import secure_filename
from flask import request, abort, send_from_directory

UPLOAD_DIR = Path('uploads')
UPLOAD_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
ALLOWED = {'pdf', 'png', 'jpg', 'jpeg'}

def allowed_file(filename):
 return '.' in filename and filename.rsplit('.', 1)[1].lower() in ALLOWED

@app.post('/upload')
def upload_file():
 file = request.files.get('document')
 if not file or file.filename == '':
 abort(400, 'No file provided')
 if not allowed_file(file.filename):
 abort(400, 'File type not allowed')

 filename = secure_filename(file.filename)
 path = UPLOAD_DIR / filename
 file.save(path)
 return {'message': 'uploaded', 'filename': filename}, 201

```

 return {'message': 'uploaded', 'filename': filename}, 201

## Serving a React build from Flask

This is useful when you deploy one backend that serves both API and React static files.

```
backend/
 app.py
 frontend_dist/
 index.html
 assets/
 index-abc123.js

```

backend/
 app.py
 frontend_dist/
 index.html
 assets/
 index-abc123.js

```
from flask import Flask, send_from_directory

app = Flask(__name__, static_folder='frontend_dist', static_url_path='')

@app.get('/api/health')
def health():
 return {'status': 'ok'}

@app.get('/')
def serve_react_index():
 return send_from_directory(app.static_folder, 'index.html')

@app.get('/<path:path>')
def serve_react_routes(path):
 # If asset exists, serve it. Otherwise return index.html for React Router.
 try:
 return send_from_directory(app.static_folder, path)
 except Exception:
 return send_from_directory(app.static_folder, 'index.html')

```

@app.get('/')
def serve_react_index():
 return send_from_directory(app.static_folder, 'index.html')

@app.get('/<path:path>')
def serve_react_routes(path):
 # If asset exists, serve it. Otherwise return index.html for React Router.
 try:
 return send_from_directory(app.static_folder, path)
 except Exception:
 return send_from_directory(app.static_folder, 'index.html')

Interview caveat: put API routes before catch-all frontend routes.

## Frontend security checklist for Flask apps

| Area | Checklist |
|---|---|
| XSS | Rely on Jinja autoescaping; avoid unsafe |safe ; sanitize rich HTML |
| CSRF | Protect cookie-auth POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE forms and AJAX |
| Cookies | Use HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite |
| Uploads | Validate size, extension, MIME if needed, scan if sensitive |
| Paths | Use secure_filename , never trust user paths |
| CORS | Do not use * with credentials in production |
| Secrets | Use environment variables, not hardcoded secrets |
| Errors | Do not leak stack traces in production |

## Flask frontend interview Q&A

Q: What is the difference between include and extends? A: extends builds a child page from a base layout. include inserts a reusable partial into the current template.

extends

include

Q: Why use macros? A: Macros keep repeated UI patterns like inputs, badges, and cards consistent.

Q: How do you serve React from Flask? A: Build React into static files, serve assets from Flask static folder, and return index.html for unknown non-API paths so React Router can handle client routes.

index.html

Q: Why is CORS needed? A: CORS is enforced by browsers when frontend and backend origins differ. It controls which origins can read responses.

# Flask and FastAPI Search, Sorting, Filtering and Pagination Implementations

Search, sorting, filtering, and pagination are extremely common interview tasks because they test frontend/backend contract design, query safety, indexes, and performance.

## API contract

```
GET /api/users?q=sha&status=active&sort=created_at&order=desc&page=1&page_size=20

```

GET /api/users?q=sha&status=active&sort=created_at&order=desc&page=1&page_size=20

Response shape:

```
{
 "items": [],
 "page": 1,
 "page_size": 20,
 "total": 153,
 "pages": 8,
 "has_next": true,
 "has_prev": false
}

```

{
 "items": [],
 "page": 1,

}

## Important rules

Concern

Rule

Search

Use indexed columns or full-text search for large datasets

Sort

Whitelist sortable fields; never directly inject query param into SQL

Pagination

Use limit/offset for simple pages; cursor pagination for large/infinite feeds

Filtering

Validate allowed filters and types

Response

Return metadata so frontend can render pagination UI

## Flask + SQLAlchemy implementation

```
from math import ceil
from flask import Blueprint, request, jsonify
from sqlalchemy import or_, asc, desc

users_bp = Blueprint('users_api', __name__, url_prefix='/api/users')

SORT_FIELDS = {
 'name': User.name,
 'email': User.email,
 'created_at': User.created_at,
}

@users_bp.get('')
def list_users():
 q = request.args.get('q', '').strip()
 status = request.args.get('status')
 sort = request.args.get('sort', 'created_at')
 order = request.args.get('order', 'desc')
 page = max(request.args.get('page', 1, type=int), 1)
 page_size = min(max(request.args.get('page_size', 20, type=int), 1), 100)

 query = User.query

 if q:
 like = f'%{q}%'
 query = query.filter(or_(User.name.ilike(like), User.email.ilike(like)))

 if status in {'active', 'inactive'}:
 query = query.filter(User.status == status)

 sort_col = SORT_FIELDS.get(sort, User.created_at)
 query = query.order_by(desc(sort_col) if order == 'desc' else asc(sort_col))

 total = query.count()
 users = query.offset((page - 1) * page_size).limit(page_size).all()

 return jsonify({
 'items': [u.to_dict() for u in users],
 'page': page,
 'page_size': page_size,
 'total': total,
 'pages': ceil(total / page_size) if page_size else 0,
 'has_next': page * page_size < total,
 'has_prev': page > 1,
 })

```

## FastAPI + SQLAlchemy implementation

```
from fastapi import APIRouter, Query, Depends
from sqlalchemy import select, func, or_, asc, desc
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import AsyncSession

router = APIRouter(prefix='/api/users', tags=['users'])

SORT_FIELDS = {
 'name': User.name,
 'email': User.email,
 'created_at': User.created_at,
}

@router.get('')
async def list_users(
 q: str = '',
 status: str | None = None,
 sort: str = 'created_at',
 order: str = 'desc',
 page: int = Query(1, ge=1),
 page_size: int = Query(20, ge=1, le=100),
 db: AsyncSession = Depends(get_db),
):
 filters = []
 if q:
 like = f'%{q}%'
 filters.append(or_(User.name.ilike(like), User.email.ilike(like)))
 if status in {'active', 'inactive'}:
 filters.append(User.status == status)

 sort_col = SORT_FIELDS.get(sort, User.created_at)
 ordering = desc(sort_col) if order == 'desc' else asc(sort_col)

 total_stmt = select(func.count()).select_from(User).where(*filters)
 total = await db.scalar(total_stmt)

 stmt = (
 select(User)
 .where(*filters)
 .order_by(ordering)
 .offset((page - 1) * page_size)
 .limit(page_size)
 )
 result = await db.execute(stmt)
 users = result.scalars().all()

 return {
 'items': [UserOut.model_validate(u).model_dump() for u in users],
 'page': page,
 'page_size': page_size,
 'total': total,
 'has_next': page * page_size < total,
 'has_prev': page > 1,
 }

```

## Cursor pagination for feeds

Offset pagination becomes slow for deep pages because the DB must skip rows. Cursor pagination uses a stable sort key like created_at plus id .

created_at

id

```
GET /api/events?limit=20&cursor=2026-06-13T10:00:00Z_891

```

GET /api/events?limit=20&cursor=2026-06-13T10:00:00Z_891

```
@router.get('/events')
async def list_events(cursor: str | None = None, limit: int = Query(20, le=100)):
 stmt = select(Event).order_by(Event.created_at.desc(), Event.id.desc()).limit(limit + 1)

 if cursor:
 cursor_created_at, cursor_id = parse_cursor(cursor)
 stmt = stmt.where(
 (Event.created_at < cursor_created_at) |
 ((Event.created_at == cursor_created_at) & (Event.id < cursor_id))
 )

 rows = (await db.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()
 has_next = len(rows) > limit
 items = rows[:limit]
 next_cursor = make_cursor(items[-1]) if has_next else None
 return {'items': items, 'next_cursor': next_cursor, 'has_next': has_next}

```

async def list_events(cursor: str | None = None, limit: int = Query(20, le=100)):
 stmt = select(Event).order_by(Event.created_at.desc(), Event.id.desc()).limit(limit + 1)

 if cursor:
 cursor_created_at, cursor_id = parse_cursor(cursor)
 stmt = stmt.where(
 (Event.created_at < cursor_created_at) |
 ((Event.created_at == cursor_created_at) & (Event.id < cursor_id))
 )

 rows = (await db.execute(stmt)).scalars().all()
 has_next = len(rows) > limit
 items = rows[:limit]
 next_cursor = make_cursor(items[-1]) if has_next else None
 return {'items': items, 'next_cursor': next_cursor, 'has_next': has_next}

## MongoDB search/sort/pagination

```
@router.get('/mongo-users')
async def mongo_users(q: str = '', page: int = 1, page_size: int = 20):
 query = {}
 if q:
 query['$or'] = [
 {'name': {'$regex': q, '$options': 'i'}},
 {'email': {'$regex': q, '$options': 'i'}},
 ]

 cursor = (
 db.users
 .find(query)
 .sort('created_at', -1)
 .skip((page - 1) * page_size)
 .limit(page_size)
 )
 items = [serialize_user(doc) async for doc in cursor]
 total = await db.users.count_documents(query)
 return {'items': items, 'total': total, 'page': page, 'page_size': page_size}

 <button disabled={!data?.has_next} onClick={() => setPage(p => p + 1)}>Next</button>
 </>
 );
}

# Sending Files from Flask and FastAPI: Upload, Download, Streaming

File handling appears simple, but production file handling involves memory, security, path traversal, MIME types, streaming, cloud storage, and cleanup.

## Flask upload and download

Upload:

```
@app.post('/api/upload')
def upload():
 file = request.files.get('file')
 if not file:
 return {'error': 'file is required'}, 400

 filename = secure_filename(file.filename)
 file.save(UPLOAD_DIR / filename)
 return {'filename': filename}, 201

```

 file.save(UPLOAD_DIR / filename)
 return {'filename': filename}, 201

Download:

```
@app.get('/api/files/<path:filename>')
def download(filename):
 return send_from_directory(UPLOAD_DIR, filename, as_attachment=True)

```

Generate and send file from memory:

```
from io import BytesIO
from flask import send_file

@app.get('/api/export.csv')
def export_csv():
 content = 'id,name\n1,Shelendra\n'
 buffer = BytesIO(content.encode('utf-8'))
 return send_file(
 buffer,
 as_attachment=True,
 download_name='users.csv',
 mimetype='text/csv'
 )

## FastAPI file upload

```
from fastapi import FastAPI, UploadFile, File, HTTPException
from pathlib import Path

app = FastAPI()
UPLOAD_DIR = Path('uploads')
UPLOAD_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

@app.post('/api/upload')
async def upload_file(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
 if file.content_type not in {'application/pdf', 'image/png', 'image/jpeg'}:
 raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail='Invalid file type')

 target = UPLOAD_DIR / file.filename
 content = await file.read()
 target.write_bytes(content)
 return {'filename': file.filename, 'size': len(content)}

 raise HTTPException(status_code=400, detail='Invalid file type')

 target.write_bytes(content)
 return {'filename': file.filename, 'size': len(content)}

For large files, avoid reading everything into memory:

```
import shutil

@app.post('/api/upload-large')
async def upload_large(file: UploadFile = File(...)):
 target = UPLOAD_DIR / file.filename
 with target.open('wb') as buffer:
 shutil.copyfileobj(file.file, buffer)
 return {'filename': file.filename}

```

 shutil.copyfileobj(file.file, buffer)
 return {'filename': file.filename}

## Security checklist

Risk

Protection

Path traversal

secure_filename , safe directories, never trust path params

secure_filename

Large upload DoS

Max content length, streaming, reverse proxy limits

Malware

Scan files if user-provided and shared

Sensitive files

Auth check before download

Public files

Use signed URLs or CDN rules

MIME spoofing

Check extension and content type; deep sniffing if needed

Storage growth

Lifecycle cleanup jobs

## Interview answer

> For small generated files I can use send_file or FileResponse . For large files I prefer streaming or object storage with signed URLs. For uploads I validate file type/size, sanitize names, avoid arbitrary paths, and store metadata in DB while actual bytes go to disk or S3-like storage.

# FastAPI Lifecycle, Lifespan, Middleware, Dependencies and Advanced Pydantic

FastAPI interviews often go beyond path/query/body params. You should understand application lifecycle, dependency injection, middleware order, validation, serialization, and how Pydantic models shape API contracts.

## FastAPI lifecycle: lifespan

Use lifespan for startup/shutdown resources such as DB pools, Redis clients, model loading, and cleanup.

```
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI
import redis.asyncio as redis

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
 app.state.redis = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
 app.state.model = load_ml_model()
 print('startup complete')
 yield
 await app.state.redis.aclose()
 print('shutdown complete')

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

```

from contextlib import asynccontextmanager
from fastapi import FastAPI
import redis.asyncio as redis

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
 app.state.redis = redis.Redis(host='localhost', port=6379, decode_responses=True)
 app.state.model = load_ml_model()
 print('startup complete')
 yield
 await app.state.redis.aclose()
 print('shutdown complete')

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

Interview line:

> Lifespan is where I initialize expensive shared resources once, then clean them up on shutdown.

Lifespan is where I initialize expensive shared resources once, then clean them up on shutdown.

## Request lifecycle mental model

```
Client request
 → ASGI server receives request
 → middleware runs before endpoint
 → dependencies resolve
 → request body is parsed and validated by Pydantic
 → endpoint function runs
 → response model serializes output
 → middleware runs after endpoint
 → response sent

```

Client request
 → ASGI server receives request
 → middleware runs before endpoint
 → dependencies resolve
 → request body is parsed and validated by Pydantic
 → endpoint function runs
 → response model serializes output
 → middleware runs after endpoint
 → response sent

## Middleware example: request ID and timing

```
import time, uuid
from fastapi import Request

@app.middleware('http')
async def add_request_context(request: Request, call_next):
 request_id = request.headers.get('X-Request-ID', str(uuid.uuid4()))
 start = time.perf_counter()

 response = await call_next(request)

 duration_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
 response.headers['X-Request-ID'] = request_id
 response.headers['X-Response-Time-ms'] = f'{duration_ms:.2f}'
 return response

```

## Field validators

```
from pydantic import field_validator

class UserCreate(BaseModel):
 email: EmailStr
 password: str

 @field_validator('password')
 @classmethod
 def password_strength(cls, value: str):
 if value.lower() == value or value.upper() == value:
 raise ValueError('Password must mix uppercase/lowercase')
 if not any(ch.isdigit() for ch in value):
 raise ValueError('Password must contain a digit')
 return value

```

## Model validators for cross-field logic

```
from pydantic import model_validator

class PasswordChange(BaseModel):
 old_password: str
 new_password: str
 confirm_password: str

 @model_validator(mode='after')
 def passwords_match(self):
 if self.new_password != self.confirm_password:
 raise ValueError('Passwords do not match')
 if self.old_password == self.new_password:
 raise ValueError('New password must be different')
 return self

```

## Background tasks vs queues

FastAPI BackgroundTasks is good for small after-response actions like logging or sending a non-critical email. For heavy/retryable/long-running work, use Celery/RQ/Arq/BullMQ/Kafka/SQS.

 return {'message': 'created'}

## Interview Q&A

Q: Where do you put DB connection setup? A: In lifespan or application factory setup, then expose sessions through dependencies.

Q: What is the advantage of dependencies? A: They centralize reusable logic like DB sessions, auth, permissions, pagination params, and services.

Q: Pydantic validator vs model validator? A: Field validators validate one field; model validators validate relationships between fields.

## Repository pattern

```
class UserRepository:
 def __init__(self, db: AsyncSession):
 self.db = db

 async def get_by_email(self, email: str) -> User | None:
 result = await self.db.execute(select(User).where(User.email == email))
 return result.scalar_one_or_none()

 async def create(self, email: str, name: str) -> User:
 user = User(email=email, name=name)
 self.db.add(user)
 await self.db.flush() # gets generated id before commit
 return user

```

 await self.db.flush() # gets generated id before commit

Service layer:

```
async def register_user(payload: UserCreate, db: AsyncSession):
 repo = UserRepository(db)
 existing = await repo.get_by_email(payload.email)
 if existing:
 raise ValueError('Email already exists')
 user = await repo.create(payload.email, payload.name)
 await db.commit()
 return user

```

## Transaction pattern

```
async with db.begin():
 order = Order(user_id=user_id, status='created')
 db.add(order)
 await db.flush()

 payment = Payment(order_id=order.id, amount=payload.amount, status='pending')
 db.add(payment)

```

async with db.begin():
 order = Order(user_id=user_id, status='created')
 db.add(order)
 await db.flush()

 payment = Payment(order_id=order.id, amount=payload.amount, status='pending')
 db.add(payment)

If an exception occurs inside the block, SQLAlchemy rolls back.

## Unit of Work pattern

```
class UnitOfWork:
 def __init__(self, session_factory):
 self.session_factory = session_factory

 async def __aenter__(self):
 self.session = self.session_factory()
 self.users = UserRepository(self.session)
 self.orders = OrderRepository(self.session)
 return self

 async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc, tb):
 if exc:
 await self.session.rollback()
 else:
 await self.session.commit()
 await self.session.close()

```

class UnitOfWork:

 self.session_factory = session_factory

 async def __aenter__(self):
 self.session = self.session_factory()
 self.users = UserRepository(self.session)
 self.orders = OrderRepository(self.session)
 return self

 async def __aexit__(self, exc_type, exc, tb):
 if exc:
 await self.session.rollback()
 else:
 await self.session.commit()
 await self.session.close()

> I prefer PostgreSQL when relationships, constraints, joins, and transactions matter. I prefer MongoDB when the access pattern is document-oriented, schema evolves quickly, or nested data is usually read/written together. In both cases, indexes should be designed from actual queries.

# Redis, BullMQ, Queues, Kafka and Event-Driven Architecture: Full Expansion

This JD directly mentions event-driven and async architectures. You must be able to explain why queues exist , what can fail, and how to design reliable processing.

## Why queues exist

Without queue:

```
API request → call payment → send email → call SMS → update DB → response waits too long

```

API request → call payment → send email → call SMS → update DB → response waits too long

With queue:

```
API request → validate → write DB → enqueue job → return fast response
Worker → process payment/email/SMS → update status → retry on failure

```

API request → validate → write DB → enqueue job → return fast response
Worker → process payment/email/SMS → update status → retry on failure

Queues provide:

Benefit

Meaning

Decoupling

API does not need to wait for slow work

Retry

Failed jobs can be retried

Smoothing spikes

Queue buffers traffic bursts

Isolation

Worker failures do not crash API

Scaling

Add more workers independently

## Queue vocabulary

Term

Meaning

Producer

Code that publishes job/message

Consumer/Worker

Code that processes job/message

Queue

Ordered-ish storage for jobs

Topic

Named event stream/channel

Retry

Run failed job again

DLQ

Dead-letter queue for permanently failed jobs

Visibility timeout

Message hidden while worker processes it

Idempotency

Same event processed twice produces safe result

## Redis as cache

```
import redis.asyncio as redis
import json

r = redis.from_url('redis://localhost:6379', decode_responses=True)

async def get_user_cached(user_id: int):
 key = f'user:{user_id}'
 cached = await r.get(key)
 if cached:
 return json.loads(cached)

 user = await db_fetch_user(user_id)
 await r.setex(key, 300, json.dumps(user))
 return user

```

import json

 cached = await r.get(key)
 if cached:
 return json.loads(cached)

 user = await db_fetch_user(user_id)
 await r.setex(key, 300, json.dumps(user))
 return user

Cache patterns:

Pattern

Flow

Cache aside

App checks cache, then DB, then fills cache

Write through

Write cache and DB together

Write behind

Write cache first, async persist later

TTL

Expire cache after time

Invalidation

Delete cache when source data changes

## Redis Pub/Sub

Pub/Sub is good for real-time broadcast where missed messages are acceptable.

```
# publisher
await r.publish('notifications', json.dumps({'user_id': 1, 'message': 'Hello'}))

```

# publisher
await r.publish('notifications', json.dumps({'user_id': 1, 'message': 'Hello'}))

```
# subscriber
pubsub = r.pubsub()
await pubsub.subscribe('notifications')
async for message in pubsub.listen():
 if message['type'] == 'message':
 print(message['data'])

```

# subscriber
pubsub = r.pubsub()
await pubsub.subscribe('notifications')
async for message in pubsub.listen():

 print(message['data'])

Use cases: websocket fanout, cache invalidation, live notifications.

Limit: normal Pub/Sub is not durable. If subscriber is down, message can be missed.

## Redis Streams

Streams are more queue-like and durable.

```

```
# consumer group setup
try:
 await r.xgroup_create('order-events', 'billing-workers', id='0', mkstream=True)
except Exception:
 pass

while True:
 messages = await r.xreadgroup(
 groupname='billing-workers',
 consumername='worker-1',
 streams={'order-events': '>'},
 count=10,
 block=5000,
 )
 for stream, events in messages:
 for event_id, data in events:
 try:
 await process_event(data)
 await r.xack('order-events', 'billing-workers', event_id)
 except Exception:
 log_exception(event_id)

## Kafka mental model

Kafka is not just a queue. It is an append-only distributed event log.

Kafka concept

Meaning

Topic

Named stream of events

Partition

Topic shard for parallelism and ordering

Producer

Writes events to topic

Consumer group

Group of consumers sharing work

Offset

Consumer’s position in a partition

Retention

Events can remain after being consumed

```
Order Service → orders topic → Billing Consumer Group
 ↘ → Analytics Consumer Group
 ↘ → Notification Consumer Group

```

Order Service → orders topic → Billing Consumer Group
 ↘ → Analytics Consumer Group
 ↘ → Notification Consumer Group

Same event can be consumed independently by multiple services.

## Event design

Good event:

```
{

 "occurred_at": "2026-06-13T10:00:00Z",
 "source": "order-service",
 "payload": {
 "order_id": "ord_123",
 "user_id": "usr_42",
 "amount": 1200
 }
}

```

{
 "event_id": "evt_123",
 "event_type": "OrderCreated",
 "occurred_at": "2026-06-13T10:00:00Z",
 "source": "order-service",
 "payload": {
 "order_id": "ord_123",
 "user_id": "usr_42",
 "amount": 1200
 }
}

Rules:

- Include event ID.

- Include type and timestamp.

- Include enough payload for consumers.

- Version your events if schema may change.

- Make consumers idempotent.

);

 await do_business_work(event)
 await db.commit()

## Outbox pattern

Problem: DB write succeeds but publishing event fails.

Solution: write business row and outbox event in same transaction.

```
CREATE TABLE outbox_events (
 id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
 event_type TEXT NOT NULL,
 payload JSONB NOT NULL,
 status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
 created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

```

CREATE TABLE outbox_events (
 id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
 event_type TEXT NOT NULL,
 payload JSONB NOT NULL,
 status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
 created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT now()
);

```
async with db.begin():
 order = Order(status='created')
 db.add(order)
 await db.flush()

 db.add(OutboxEvent(
 event_type='OrderCreated',
 payload={'order_id': order.id}
 ))

```

async with db.begin():
 order = Order(status='created')
 db.add(order)
 await db.flush()

 db.add(OutboxEvent(
 event_type='OrderCreated',
 payload={'order_id': order.id}
 ))

A separate publisher reads pending outbox rows and publishes to Kafka/SQS/Redis.

# Python Scheduled Jobs: APScheduler, Celery Beat, Cron and Cloud Schedulers

Scheduled jobs are used for reports, reminders, cleanup, syncing third-party data, retrying pending webhooks, cache warming, and billing tasks.

## Options

Option

Best for

Cron

Simple server-level scripts

APScheduler

In-process Python scheduling

Celery Beat

Distributed scheduled jobs with Celery workers

RQ Scheduler

Redis Queue scheduled jobs

Arq

Async Python Redis jobs

Cloud Scheduler/EventBridge

Cloud-native scheduled triggers

Kubernetes CronJob

Containerized scheduled tasks

## Simple cron script

```
# run every day at 2:30 AM
30 2 * * * /usr/bin/python3 /app/jobs/cleanup_old_sessions.py >> /var/log/app-cleanup.log 2>&1

```

# run every day at 2:30 AM
30 2 * * * /usr/bin/python3 /app/jobs/cleanup_old_sessions.py >> /var/log/app-cleanup.log 2>&1

Good for simple deployments. Weakness: not very visible unless logging/monitoring is configured.

## APScheduler example

```
from apscheduler.schedulers.asyncio import AsyncIOScheduler
from fastapi import FastAPI
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

scheduler = AsyncIOScheduler()

async def sync_pending_webhooks():
 print('sync pending webhooks')

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
 scheduler.add_job(sync_pending_webhooks, 'interval', minutes=5, id='webhook-sync')
 scheduler.start()
 yield
 scheduler.shutdown()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

```

from apscheduler.schedulers.asyncio import AsyncIOScheduler
from fastapi import FastAPI
from contextlib import asynccontextmanager

scheduler = AsyncIOScheduler()

async def sync_pending_webhooks():
 print('sync pending webhooks')

@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
 scheduler.add_job(sync_pending_webhooks, 'interval', minutes=5, id='webhook-sync')
 scheduler.start()
 yield
 scheduler.shutdown()

app = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)

Caution: if you run multiple app instances, each instance may start the same scheduler. For production, isolate scheduler into one process or use distributed locking.

celery_app = Celery('app', broker='redis://localhost:6379/0')

def generate_daily_report():
 print('report generated')

celery_app.conf.beat_schedule = {
 'daily-report': {
 'task': 'generate_daily_report',
 'schedule': 60 * 60 * 24,
 }
}

## FastAPI endpoint enqueueing scheduled-like delayed work

```
@app.post('/reminders')
async def create_reminder(payload: ReminderCreate):
 reminder = await save_reminder(payload)
 await queue.enqueue_at(payload.remind_at, 'send_reminder', reminder.id)
 return {'id': reminder.id}

```

 return {'id': reminder.id}

## Scheduled job reliability checklist

- Make jobs idempotent.

- Store job status in DB.

- Use locking if only one instance should run it.

- Add retry/backoff.

- Add timeout.

- Log start/end/duration/errors.

- Alert if job fails repeatedly.

- Avoid huge jobs; batch work.

- Use cursor/checkpoint for long syncs.

- Make reruns safe.

# React Lifecycle Properly: Render, Commit, Effects, Cleanup and Strict Mode

Modern React lifecycle is mostly explained through render , commit , and effects , not class lifecycle methods.

## Mental model

```
State/props/context change
 → React calls component function to calculate UI
 → React compares new tree with previous tree
 → React commits minimal DOM changes
 → Browser paints
 → Effects run after paint

```

State/props/context change
 → React calls component function to calculate UI
 → React compares new tree with previous tree
 → React commits minimal DOM changes
 → Browser paints
 → Effects run after paint

## Render phase

The component function runs. This phase should be pure.

```
function UserCard({ user }: { user: User }) {
 // Good: calculate derived value
 const fullName = `${user.firstName} ${user.lastName}`;

 // Bad: API call directly inside render
 // fetch('/api/log-view');

 return <h2>{fullName}</h2>;
}

```

function UserCard({ user }: { user: User }) {
 // Good: calculate derived value
 const fullName = `${user.firstName} ${user.lastName}`;

 // Bad: API call directly inside render
 // fetch('/api/log-view');

 return <h2>{fullName}</h2>;
}

Do not mutate state, perform API calls, attach listeners, or write to localStorage directly during render.

## Commit phase

React applies changes to the DOM. Refs are assigned during commit.

```
const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement | null>(null);

useEffect(() => {
 inputRef.current?.focus();
}, []);

```

const inputRef = useRef<HTMLInputElement | null>(null);

useEffect(() => {
 inputRef.current?.focus();
}, []);

## Effects lifecycle

```
useEffect(() => {
 console.log('effect runs after render commit');

 return () => {
 console.log('cleanup runs before effect re-runs or component unmounts');
 };
}, [dependency]);

```

useEffect(() => {
 console.log('effect runs after render commit');

 return () => {
 console.log('cleanup runs before effect re-runs or component unmounts');
 };
}, [dependency]);

Effect scenarios:

Dependency array

Meaning

No array

Runs after every render

[]

[]

Runs after initial mount; cleanup on unmount

[x]

[x]

Runs when x changes

x

## Fetching data with cleanup

```
function UserPage({ id }: { id: string }) {
 const [user, setUser] = useState<User | null>(null);
 const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false);

 useEffect(() => {
 const controller = new AbortController();

 async function loadUser() {
 setLoading(true);
 try {
 const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`, { signal: controller.signal });
 const data = await res.json();
 setUser(data);
 } catch (err) {
 if ((err as Error).name !== 'AbortError') console.error(err);
 } finally {
 setLoading(false);
 }
 }

 loadUser();
 return () => controller.abort();
 }, [id]);

 if (loading) return <p>Loading...</p>;
 return <pre>{JSON.stringify(user, null, 2)}</pre>;
}

```

function UserPage({ id }: { id: string }) {
 const [user, setUser] = useState<User | null>(null);
 const [loading, setLoading] = useState(false);

 useEffect(() => {
 const controller = new AbortController();

 async function loadUser() {
 setLoading(true);
 try {
 const res = await fetch(`/api/users/${id}`, { signal: controller.signal });
 const data = await res.json();
 setUser(data);
 } catch (err) {
 if ((err as Error).name !== 'AbortError') console.error(err);
 } finally {
 setLoading(false);
 }
 }

 loadUser();
 return () => controller.abort();
 }, [id]);

 if (loading) return <p>Loading...</p>;
 return <pre>{JSON.stringify(user, null, 2)}</pre>;
}

useLayoutEffect

Runs

Used for

After browser paint

useLayoutEffect

## Class lifecycle mapping

Class lifecycle

Hook equivalent

componentDidMount

componentDidUpdate

useEffect(..., [deps])

## Strict Mode double effect behavior

In development, React Strict Mode may run effects twice to help expose unsafe side effects. Your effect should tolerate setup → cleanup → setup.

Bad:

```
useEffect(() => {
 socket.connect();
}, []); // no cleanup

```

useEffect(() => {
 socket.connect();
}, []); // no cleanup

Good:

```
useEffect(() => {
 socket.connect();
 return () => socket.disconnect();
}, []);

```

 socket.connect();
 return () => socket.disconnect();
}, []);

## Common lifecycle mistakes

Mistake

Fix

API call inside render

Put it in effect or RTK Query

Missing dependency

Add dependency or restructure logic

Infinite effect loop

Avoid setting dependency inside effect unnecessarily

Derived state in effect

Calculate during render if possible

No cleanup

Return cleanup for listeners, timers, sockets, fetch abort

## Interview answer

> In functional React, lifecycle is render, commit, and effects. Render must stay pure. Effects synchronize with external systems like APIs, timers, sockets, or DOM subscriptions. Cleanup prevents leaks and stale updates.

# React Virtual DOM, Reconciliation, Keys, Memoization and IntersectionObserver

This section helps you answer deeper React questions that separate “I can write components” from “I understand how React updates UI.”

## Virtual DOM in simple words

The virtual DOM is a lightweight representation of the UI tree. When state changes, React creates a new UI description and compares it with the previous one to decide what real DOM changes are needed.

```
State change
 → new React element tree
 → compare with old tree
 → calculate minimal changes
 → update actual DOM

```

State change
 → new React element tree
 → compare with old tree
 → calculate minimal changes
 → update actual DOM

Important: React does not magically make everything fast. Bad rendering patterns can still be slow.

## Reconciliation rules

React uses heuristics:

- Different element types produce different trees.

- Same component type can be updated with new props.

- Keys help React match list items.

Bad keys:

```
{items.map((item, index) => (
 <TodoRow key={index} item={item} />
))}

```

{items.map((item, index) => (
 <TodoRow key={index} item={item} />
))}

Good keys:

```
{items.map(item => (
 <TodoRow key={item.id} item={item} />
))}

```

{items.map(item => (
 <TodoRow key={item.id} item={item} />
))}

Index keys can break state when list order changes.

## Why components re-render

A component can re-render when:

- Its state changes.

- Its parent re-renders.

- Context value it uses changes.

- Redux selector result changes.

- External store subscription changes.

## React.memo

React.memo

```
const TodoRow = React.memo(function TodoRow({ todo, onToggle }: Props) {
 return <li onClick={() => onToggle(todo.id)}>{todo.title}</li>;
});

```

const TodoRow = React.memo(function TodoRow({ todo, onToggle }: Props) {
 return <li onClick={() => onToggle(todo.id)}>{todo.title}</li>;
});

memo helps only if props are stable. If parent creates a new function/object every render, memo may not help.

memo

## useCallback

useCallback

```
const handleToggle = useCallback((id: string) => {
 dispatch(toggleTodo(id));
}, [dispatch]);

```

const handleToggle = useCallback((id: string) => {
 dispatch(toggleTodo(id));
}, [dispatch]);

## useMemo

useMemo

```
const filtered = useMemo(() => {
 return todos.filter(todo => todo.title.toLowerCase().includes(q.toLowerCase()));
}, [todos, q]);

```

const filtered = useMemo(() => {
 return todos.filter(todo => todo.title.toLowerCase().includes(q.toLowerCase()));
}, [todos, q]);

Use memoization when computation is expensive or referential stability matters. Do not wrap everything blindly.

## Debounced search hook

```
function useDebounce<T>(value: T, delay = 400): T {
 const [debounced, setDebounced] = useState(value);

 useEffect(() => {
 const id = window.setTimeout(() => setDebounced(value), delay);
 return () => window.clearTimeout(id);
 }, [value, delay]);

 return debounced;
}

```

function useDebounce<T>(value: T, delay = 400): T {
 const [debounced, setDebounced] = useState(value);

 useEffect(() => {
 const id = window.setTimeout(() => setDebounced(value), delay);
 return () => window.clearTimeout(id);
 }, [value, delay]);

 return debounced;
}

## IntersectionObserver for infinite scroll

IntersectionObserver observes whether an element enters the viewport. It is better than listening to scroll events manually.

```
function InfiniteUsers() {
 const [page, setPage] = useState(1);
 const sentinelRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement | null>(null);
 const { data, isFetching } = useGetUsersQuery({ page });

 useEffect(() => {
 const node = sentinelRef.current;
 if (!node) return;

 const observer = new IntersectionObserver(entries => {
 const first = entries[0];
 if (first.isIntersecting && !isFetching && data?.has_next) {
 setPage(p => p + 1);
 }
 }, { rootMargin: '200px' });

 observer.observe(node);
 return () => observer.disconnect();
 }, [isFetching, data?.has_next]);

 return (
 <>
 {data?.items.map(user => <div key={user.id}>{user.name}</div>)}
 <div ref={sentinelRef}>Load more trigger</div>
 </>
 );
}

```

function InfiniteUsers() {
 const [page, setPage] = useState(1);
 const sentinelRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement | null>(null);
 const { data, isFetching } = useGetUsersQuery({ page });

 useEffect(() => {
 const node = sentinelRef.current;
 if (!node) return;

 const observer = new IntersectionObserver(entries => {
 const first = entries[0];
 if (first.isIntersecting && !isFetching && data?.has_next) {
 setPage(p => p + 1);
 }
 }, { rootMargin: '200px' });

 observer.observe(node);
 return () => observer.disconnect();
 }, [isFetching, data?.has_next]);

 return (
 <>
 {data?.items.map(user => <div key={user.id}>{user.name}</div>)}
 <div ref={sentinelRef}>Load more trigger</div>
 </>
 );
}

Use cases:

Use case

Why IntersectionObserver helps

Infinite scroll

Trigger next page when sentinel appears

Lazy image loading

Load image when near viewport

Analytics

Track whether section was viewed

Animations

Start animation when element appears

# Redux Lifecycle Properly: Store, Dispatch, Reducers, Middleware, Async Thunks and RTK Query

Redux lifecycle means understanding what happens from a UI event to state update and re-render.

## Core Redux data flow

```
User action in component
 → dispatch(action)
 → middleware may intercept
 → reducer calculates next state
 → store saves next state
 → subscribed components re-select state
 → React re-renders affected components

```

User action in component
 → dispatch(action)
 → middleware may intercept
 → reducer calculates next state
 → store saves next state
 → subscribed components re-select state
 → React re-renders affected components

## Slice example

```
import { createSlice, PayloadAction } from '@reduxjs/toolkit';

type Todo = { id: string; title: string; done: boolean };

type TodosState = {
 items: Todo[];
 filter: 'all' | 'active' | 'done';
};

const initialState: TodosState = { items: [], filter: 'all' };

const todosSlice = createSlice({
 name: 'todos',
 initialState,
 reducers: {
 addTodo(state, action: PayloadAction<{ title: string }>) {
 state.items.push({
 id: crypto.randomUUID(),
 title: action.payload.title,
 done: false,
 });
 },
 toggleTodo(state, action: PayloadAction<string>) {
 const todo = state.items.find(t => t.id === action.payload);
 if (todo) todo.done = !todo.done;
 },
 setFilter(state, action: PayloadAction<TodosState['filter']>) {
 state.filter = action.payload;
 },
 },
});

export const { addTodo, toggleTodo, setFilter } = todosSlice.actions;
export default todosSlice.reducer;

```

```
export const selectTodos = (state: RootState) => state.todos.items;
export const selectFilter = (state: RootState) => state.todos.filter;

export const selectVisibleTodos = createSelector(
 [selectTodos, selectFilter],
 (todos, filter) => {
 if (filter === 'active') return todos.filter(t => !t.done);
 if (filter === 'done') return todos.filter(t => t.done);
 return todos;
 }
);

 return await res.json();

```
const usersSlice = createSlice({
 name: 'users',
 initialState: { items: [], status: 'idle', error: null } as UsersState,
 reducers: {},
 extraReducers: builder => {
 builder
 .addCase(fetchUsers.pending, state => {
 state.status = 'loading';
 })
 .addCase(fetchUsers.fulfilled, (state, action) => {
 state.status = 'succeeded';
 state.items = action.payload;
 })
 .addCase(fetchUsers.rejected, (state, action) => {
 state.status = 'failed';
 state.error = action.payload ?? action.error.message;
 });
 },
});

## RTK Query lifecycle

RTK Query manages request status, caching, deduping, refetching, and invalidation.

```
import { createApi, fetchBaseQuery } from '@reduxjs/toolkit/query/react';

export const api = createApi({
 reducerPath: 'api',
 baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({
 baseUrl: '/api',
 prepareHeaders: (headers, { getState }) => {
 const token = (getState() as RootState).auth.token;
 if (token) headers.set('authorization', `Bearer ${token}`);
 return headers;
 },
 }),
 tagTypes: ['User'],
 endpoints: builder => ({
 getUsers: builder.query<User[], void>({
 query: () => '/users',
 providesTags: ['User'],
 }),
 createUser: builder.mutation<User, Partial<User>>({
 query: body => ({ url: '/users', method: 'POST', body }),
 invalidatesTags: ['User'],
 }),
 }),
});

export const { useGetUsersQuery, useCreateUserMutation } = api;

```

# Node.js Touch-Grass Chapter: Express/Nest Basics for a Python Full Stack Developer

The JD prefers Node.js/NestJS/Express but says Python is a plus. You do not need to become a Node expert overnight, but you should be able to speak the basics.

## Node.js mental model

Node.js runs JavaScript on the server using an event loop. It is very strong for I/O-heavy APIs: HTTP requests, DB calls, queues, file operations, and real-time apps.

```
Request arrives
 → callback/promise registered
 → I/O begins
 → event loop continues handling other work
 → I/O completes
 → callback/promise continuation runs

```

Request arrives
 → callback/promise registered
 → I/O begins

 → I/O completes
 → callback/promise continuation runs

import express from 'express';

app.use(express.json());

app.post('/users', async (req, res) => {
 const { email, name } = req.body;
 if (!email) return res.status(400).json({ error: 'email required' });
 const user = await createUser({ email, name });
 res.status(201).json(user);
});

app.listen(3000, () => console.log('listening on 3000'));

## Express middleware

```
function requestLogger(req, res, next) {
 console.log(req.method, req.url);
 next();
}

function requireAuth(req, res, next) {
 const token = req.headers.authorization?.replace('Bearer ', '');
 if (!token) return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Unauthorized' });
 req.user = verifyToken(token);
 next();
}

app.use(requestLogger);
app.get('/me', requireAuth, (req, res) => res.json(req.user));

Pipe → validation/transformation
Interceptor → wrapping response/logging

# AWS Scaling Scenarios: Lambda, SQS, Glue and Event-Driven Pipelines

You have real AWS exposure, so this section helps you convert it into interview-ready architecture language.

## Lambda scaling mental model

Lambda scales by running more concurrent function instances when more events arrive, within account/function concurrency limits and event source behavior.

```
API Gateway / S3 / SQS / EventBridge
 → Lambda invocation
 → function instance handles one event/batch
 → more traffic creates more concurrent instances

```

API Gateway / S3 / SQS / EventBridge
 → Lambda invocation
 → function instance handles one event/batch
 → more traffic creates more concurrent instances

Key terms:

Term

Meaning

Cold start

New execution environment initialization

Concurrency

Number of function instances running at same time

Reserved concurrency

Guaranteed/max concurrency for a function

Provisioned concurrency

Pre-warmed execution environments

Timeout

Max execution time

DLQ/destination

Failure routing

## SQS + Lambda flow

```
Producer writes message to SQS
 → Lambda event source mapping polls queue
 → Lambda receives batch of messages
 → function processes each message
 → successful batch messages are deleted
 → failed messages become visible again or go DLQ after max receives

```

Producer writes message to SQS
 → Lambda event source mapping polls queue
 → Lambda receives batch of messages
 → function processes each message
 → successful batch messages are deleted
 → failed messages become visible again or go DLQ after max receives

Python handler:

```
import json

def handler(event, context):
 failures = []
 for record in event['Records']:
 message_id = record['messageId']
 try:
 body = json.loads(record['body'])
 process_message(body)
 except Exception:
 failures.append({'itemIdentifier': message_id})

 # partial batch response pattern
 return {'batchItemFailures': failures}

```

import json

def handler(event, context):
 failures = []
 for record in event['Records']:
 message_id = record['messageId']
 try:
 body = json.loads(record['body'])
 process_message(body)
 except Exception:
 failures.append({'itemIdentifier': message_id})

 # partial batch response pattern
 return {'batchItemFailures': failures}

Why partial batch matters: one bad message should not force successful messages to be retried repeatedly.

## SQS design checklist

Concern

Design

Duplicate processing

Idempotency key/table

Poison messages

DLQ with maxReceiveCount

Long processing

Visibility timeout > max processing time

Throughput

Batch size, concurrency, multiple workers

Ordering

FIFO queue with message group ID

Backpressure

Queue depth alarms

## Lambda + S3 file processing scenario

```
User uploads PDF to S3
 → S3 event triggers Lambda
 → Lambda validates metadata
 → writes job message to SQS
 → worker Lambda processes file
 → stores result in S3
 → updates DB status

```

User uploads PDF to S3
 → S3 event triggers Lambda
 → Lambda validates metadata
 → writes job message to SQS
 → worker Lambda processes file
 → stores result in S3
 → updates DB status

Why not do everything in S3-trigger Lambda? Because direct S3 events can spike, retries can duplicate, and long processing may hit timeout. SQS buffers and controls processing.

## AWS Glue basics

AWS Glue is used for ETL/data integration: crawling metadata, transforming data, and writing into data lakes/warehouses.

```
Raw files in S3
 → Glue crawler updates catalog
 → Glue job transforms data
 → curated data written to S3/Redshift/Postgres
 → Athena/BI/API consumes data

```

Raw files in S3
 → Glue crawler updates catalog
 → Glue job transforms data
 → curated data written to S3/Redshift/Postgres
 → Athena/BI/API consumes data

Glue job concepts:

Concept

Meaning

Crawler

Infers schema and updates Data Catalog

Data Catalog

Metadata store for datasets

Job

ETL script, often Spark-based

Trigger

Runs job on schedule/event

Bookmark

Tracks processed data to avoid reprocessing

## Scaling ETL scenario

Problem: millions of records arrive daily and API should not process them synchronously.

Architecture:

```
Upload batch file to S3
 → S3 event/EventBridge
 → SQS message
 → Lambda validates and starts Glue job
 → Glue transforms large data
 → writes curated output
 → Lambda/EventBridge updates status

```

Upload batch file to S3
 → S3 event/EventBridge
 → SQS message
 → Lambda validates and starts Glue job
 → Glue transforms large data
 → writes curated output
 → Lambda/EventBridge updates status

## Interview answer

> I would use Lambda for event-driven lightweight compute, SQS for buffering/retry/backpressure, DLQ for poison messages, and Glue for heavier ETL transformations. For file pipelines, I avoid doing long work directly in request-response APIs and use status tracking plus async processing.

I would use Lambda for event-driven lightweight compute, SQS for buffering/retry/backpressure, DLQ for poison messages, and Glue for heavier ETL transformations. For file pipelines, I avoid doing long work directly in request-response APIs and use status tracking plus async processing.

# SQS Queuing Deep Dive: Visibility Timeout, DLQ, FIFO, Idempotency and Lambda Consumers

SQS is one of the easiest queues to explain well in interviews because the lifecycle is concrete.

## Message lifecycle

```
Producer sends message
 → message is visible in queue
 → consumer receives message
 → message becomes invisible for visibility timeout
 → consumer deletes message after success
 → if not deleted, message becomes visible again
 → after repeated failures, message goes to DLQ

```

Producer sends message
 → message is visible in queue
 → consumer receives message
 → message becomes invisible for visibility timeout
 → consumer deletes message after success
 → if not deleted, message becomes visible again
 → after repeated failures, message goes to DLQ

## Standard vs FIFO

Queue type

Behavior

Standard

High throughput, at-least-once delivery, best-effort ordering

FIFO

Ordering per message group, exactly-once processing support through deduplication window

Use FIFO when strict order matters, such as wallet transactions per user.

## Producer example with boto3

```
import boto3, json, uuid

sqs = boto3.client('sqs')

sqs.send_message(
 QueueUrl=QUEUE_URL,
 MessageBody=json.dumps({
 'event_id': str(uuid.uuid4()),
 'type': 'InvoiceRequested',
 'invoice_id': 123,
 })
)

```

FIFO producer:

```
sqs.send_message(
 QueueUrl=FIFO_QUEUE_URL,
 MessageBody=json.dumps(payload),
 MessageGroupId=f'user-{user_id}',
 MessageDeduplicationId=payload['event_id'],
)

```

sqs.send_message(
 QueueUrl=FIFO_QUEUE_URL,
 MessageBody=json.dumps(payload),
 MessageGroupId=f'user-{user_id}',
 MessageDeduplicationId=payload['event_id'],
)

## Lambda consumer with idempotency

```
def process_invoice(event):
 event_id = event['event_id']

 if already_processed(event_id):
 return

 create_invoice(event['invoice_id'])
 mark_processed(event_id)

```

def process_invoice(event):
 event_id = event['event_id']

 if already_processed(event_id):
 return

 create_invoice(event['invoice_id'])
 mark_processed(event_id)

## Partial batch failure response

```
def handler(event, context):
 batch_failures = []

 for record in event['Records']:
 try:
 process_invoice(json.loads(record['body']))
 except Exception:
 batch_failures.append({'itemIdentifier': record['messageId']})

 return {'batchItemFailures': batch_failures}

```

def handler(event, context):
 batch_failures = []

 for record in event['Records']:
 try:
 process_invoice(json.loads(record['body']))
 except Exception:
 batch_failures.append({'itemIdentifier': record['messageId']})

 return {'batchItemFailures': batch_failures}

## Visibility timeout rule

Set visibility timeout longer than max processing time.

```
processing takes 60 seconds
visibility timeout is 30 seconds
→ same message may be delivered to another worker before first worker finishes

```

processing takes 60 seconds
visibility timeout is 30 seconds
→ same message may be delivered to another worker before first worker finishes

## DLQ rule

After maxReceiveCount , move bad messages to DLQ.

maxReceiveCount

```
Main queue maxReceiveCount = 5
Message fails 5 receives
→ goes to dead-letter queue
→ alert + inspect + replay after fix

```

Main queue maxReceiveCount = 5
Message fails 5 receives
→ goes to dead-letter queue
→ alert + inspect + replay after fix

## Backpressure

If queue depth grows:

- Increase worker concurrency if downstream can handle it.

- Optimize processing time.

- Batch messages.

- Shed load or throttle producers.

- Protect DB/third-party APIs from overload.

## Interview answer

> SQS provides at-least-once delivery, so I design consumers to be idempotent. I tune visibility timeout, batch size, concurrency, and DLQ. With Lambda, I prefer partial batch failure response so one bad message does not retry the whole batch.

SQS provides at-least-once delivery, so I design consumers to be idempotent. I tune visibility timeout, batch size, concurrency, and DLQ. With Lambda, I prefer partial batch failure response so one bad message does not retry the whole batch.

# Mini Project Route Map for This JD

Build one project that proves the exact JD skills: React frontend, Flask/FastAPI APIs, auth, PostgreSQL/MongoDB, Redis queue/cache, file upload, webhooks, async workers, search/sort/pagination, and deployment basics.

> I built a ticketing dashboard where React handles protected routes, search/sort/pagination, and file uploads. The API validates requests, persists data in PostgreSQL, stores files separately, publishes jobs to a queue, and processes notifications/webhooks asynchronously with idempotency and retries.
